title: The part that goes silent during arguments and regrets it later
author: Kalesh
excerpt: You know the feeling. The argument is happening, and suddenly your mouth is glued shut. Words are there, lined up in your head, but they can't make it past you...
metaDescription: You know the feeling. The argument is happening, and suddenly your mouth is glued shut. Words are there, lined up in your head, but they can't make it pas...
htmlContent:
You know the feeling. The argument is happening, and suddenly your mouth is glued shut. Words are there, lined up in your head, but they can't make it past your throat. You're frozen. Silent. And later, alone in the bathroom or staring at the ceiling at 2 AM, the regret hits like a truck. You should have said something. You should have defended yourself. You should have spoken up.

I've been there. More times than I can count. And for years I thought it was a character flaw. I thought I was weak. I thought I was just someone who couldn't handle conflict. But here's the thing: that silence isn't weakness. It's a part of you. A part that learned, somewhere along the way, that speaking was dangerous.

The part that learned silence was survival

Let's get specific about what's happening in your nervous system when you go quiet during an argument. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, would tell you that your body is doing exactly what it evolved to do. When your nervous system detects threat, it has three options: fight, flight, or freeze. That silence? That's freeze.

Your body is saying, "If I stay very still and very quiet, maybe the danger will pass." It's a strategy that worked for you once. Maybe it worked when you were a kid and a parent's anger was unpredictable. Maybe it worked when a teacher's criticism felt like an attack. Maybe it worked in a relationship where speaking your truth got you punished.

The part that goes silent isn't stupid. It's smart. It's the part that kept you safe when you couldn't protect yourself. But here's the problem: that part is still running the show in situations where you're actually safe now. Your partner isn't going to hit you. Your boss isn't going to fire you for disagreeing. Your friend isn't going to abandon you for having a different opinion. But your nervous system doesn't know that. It's still operating from old data.

Wild, right? Your body is living in 2024 but running threat assessments from 1998.

Why regret comes later

So you go silent. The argument ends. And then the flood comes. The words you couldn't say start looping in your head. You rehearse what you should have said. You feel the anger, the frustration, the hurt that was locked away during the freeze response. And now it's too late. The moment is gone.

Let me tell you about the protector parts. The ones that look like anger, or control, or perfectionism, or emotional walls. In IFS, we call them managers and firefighters. Managers try to prevent pain by controlling everything. Firefighters try to extinguish pain after it's already been triggered - through numbing, bingeing, dissociating, raging.

I used to hate my protector parts. The part that made me cold and distant. The part that made me work until I collapsed. The part that drank too much. I thought they were the problem. Turns out they were the solution - the best solution my system could find at the time. They kept me alive when nothing else could.

Notice if there's a part of you that's skeptical right now. A part that says "this is too simple" or "this won't work for me" or "I'm too damaged for this." That's a protector too. It's trying to save you from hope, because hope has been dangerous before. It's been followed by disappointment too many times. Can you thank that part? Can you say "I get it. You're trying to protect me from getting hurt again." That's not weakness. That's the beginning of internal trust.

This is where Janina Fisher's work on trauma and the self is so helpful. She talks about how trauma survivors often have parts that hold different pieces of experience. One part holds the fear and the silence. Another part holds the anger and the words that never got said. And when the silent part takes over, the angry part gets locked in a cage. Later, when the threat passes, the angry part comes out and starts beating you up for not speaking.

So now you're dealing with two kinds of pain. The original pain of the argument. And the secondary pain of self-criticism for not handling it better. That's a heavy load. And it's one of the reasons why recovery from complex trauma is so exhausting. You're not just healing from the past. You're healing from the way you learned to cope with the past.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. She lays out how trauma changes the way we relate to ourselves and others. Reading it helped me understand that my silence wasn't a personal failing. It was a predictable response to an unpredictable history.

I want you to sit with that for a second. Your silence isn't a sign that you're broken. It's a sign that you survived something. And that survival strategy, while it may not serve you now, deserves compassion, not contempt.

How to start working with the silent part

Here's where we get practical. Because knowing why you go silent is one thing. Actually changing the pattern is another. And I'm not going to tell you that you can just "choose to speak up" or " discomfort." That's toxic positivity dressed up as advice. It doesn't work for trauma survivors because it ignores the nervous system reality.

So what does work? Slow, patient, iterative work with the part that goes silent. Richard Schwartz, the creator of Internal Family Systems (IFS), calls this "parts work." The idea is that every part of you, even the one that shuts down during arguments, has a positive intention. It's trying to protect you. Your job isn't to force it to be quiet. Your job is to get to know it.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. It walks you through how to talk to your parts with curiosity instead of judgment. And that shift, from judgment to curiosity, is everything.

Here's a practice I use with my own silent part. It's simple but not easy.

First, I notice when the silence is happening. Not in the middle of the argument, because that's too late. My nervous system is already locked in. But after, when the regret comes. I sit with the feeling and I ask: "Who went quiet?" Not "why did I go quiet" but "who went quiet." That small language shift opens up space.

Then I listen. Not for a logical explanation, but for a feeling. Maybe it's a young part that feels small and scared. Maybe it's a teenager who learned that speaking got them in trouble. Maybe it's a part that holds the memory of being silenced by someone who should have protected them.

I don't try to change this part. I don't tell it to "speak up next time." I just let it know I see it. I thank it for keeping me safe. And I promise that we'll figure this out together, slowly, over time.

This is not a quick fix. You won't read this article and suddenly start winning arguments. But you might start noticing the silence with a little less shame. And that's a win. That's the foundation for real change.

What to do when you can't speak in the moment

Look, there will be times when you're in an argument and the freeze hits before you can stop it. That's going to happen. And when it does, you need a plan that works with your nervous system, not against it.

Here's what I've learned from Peter Levine's work on Somatic Experiencing. The freeze response isn't just psychological. It's physical. Your muscles tense. Your breath gets shallow. Your eyes might go unfocused. So the way out has to be physical too.

When I feel the silence coming, I try to do one small physical thing. I press my feet into the floor. I take one slow breath. I shift my weight from one foot to the other. These tiny movements send a signal to my nervous system that I'm not actually stuck. I can move. And movement is the opposite of freeze.

If I can't speak, I sometimes say: "I need a minute. I'm having a reaction and I want to respond, not react." This buys me time. And it's honest. I'm not pretending to be fine. I'm telling the truth about what's happening in my body.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link) by Janina Fisher. She offers specific tools for working with the parts that get activated in conflict. It's dense, but it's worth the effort.

After the argument, when the regret comes, I do something different. Instead of beating myself up, I write down what I wanted to say. Not to rehearse for next time, but to let that angry, frustrated part have its voice. I let it say everything. And then I ask: "What would it have cost me to say that in the moment?"

The answer is often revealing. Sometimes it would have cost me safety. Sometimes it would have cost me the relationship. Sometimes it would have cost me nothing but my fear. And knowing the difference helps me work with the silent part more skillfully.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) by Arielle Schwartz. It has exercises for tracking your triggers and building new responses. I've used it myself and found it grounding.

The slow path to a different response

I'm not going to tell you that you'll never go silent again. That's not how trauma recovery works. But I will tell you that the silence can change. It can go from a wall of shame to a moment of pause. It can go from a locked door to a door you choose not to open, because you know what's on the other side.

The part that goes silent during arguments and regrets it later is not your enemy. It's not a flaw. It's a part of you that learned, in a very real and painful way, that silence was safer than speech. And that part deserves your gratitude, not your scorn.

But it also deserves your leadership. You're the adult now. You have resources that part never had. You have support, knowledge, and a growing understanding of how your nervous system works. You can start to show that part that it's okay to take risks. That you'll be there if it goes wrong. That it doesn't have to carry the burden of survival alone anymore.

This is slow work. It's not linear. You'll have good days and bad days. Days when you speak up and feel powerful. Days when you go silent and feel defeated. But every time you notice the silence with compassion instead of contempt, you're rewiring something. You're telling your nervous system that this part is welcome. That it's not a problem to be fixed. That it's a part of you, and you're learning to hold all of your parts with care.

And that, honestly, is the real work. Not learning to win arguments. Not learning to never freeze again. But learning to be with yourself, in all your complexity, without judgment. That's the path. And it's worth walking.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) by Arielle Schwartz
  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link) by Janina Fisher
